solo.name: Alucard Chonokoji
solo.quirk_summary: BLACK allows the user to fuse with any dark-coloured object or shadow, natural or artificial. While merged, the user becomes one with the darkness — invisible, soundless, able to move through solid matter freely. At base level it grants +20 movement speed while merged and the ability to issue Dark Command: a short-range compulsion that forces a target to act under the user's influence. The weakness is bright light, which forcibly ejects the user. Alucard has used this quirk for long enough that its limits are not what the compendium describes. He does not need a large shadow. He has been observed merging with the shadow cast by a single finger. He has been observed inside a room with no shadows at all. The Commission file notes this discrepancy and offers no explanation.
solo.quirk_mechanics_ref: BLACK — Quirk Compendium p.26. Type: Transformation. Base abilities: Shadow Merge (bonus action/reaction, merge with shadow/dark object within 30ft, invisible + silent, pass through solid matter, +20 speed, exit as free action), Dark Command (action, control creature or dark object within 10ft, WIS save vs Quirk DC, 2 rounds). Weakness: bright light ejects user, requires nearby dark surface. Alucard's version is scaled well beyond compendium baseline — treat special abilities below as his mastered form.
solo.appearance: Tall. Lean in the way that suggests the mass is somewhere else. Dark hair, long enough to fall across his face, which it often does. Pale — the specific pale of someone who spends no time in sunlight, not as a stylistic choice but as an apparent condition. Wears black, always. Not a costume. Not armour. Just black clothing that fits with no visible seams. His eyes are dark brown — this is always slightly surprising. People expect something stranger and the ordinariness of it is worse. He moves without sound. Not quietly. Without sound. A deliberate footstep makes no more noise than a thought. When he uses Shadow Merge he does not vanish dramatically — he steps sideways into a shadow and is simply gone, the way a hand disappears into still water.
solo.personality: He does not perform. He does not posture. He does not monologue. He speaks when he has something to say and in those cases uses the exact number of words required. He is not warm. He is not kind. He is also not an enemy — he simply operates by a different set of rules than anyone the class has met. The most significant thing about him is that he chose to be noticed. He could have watched the class indefinitely without them knowing. He didn't. That decision means something, and the class will spend a long time working out what.
solo.combat_style: When called via the three-name summoning he arrives in the nearest shadow to the crisis and assesses before acting. He will not take direction. He will not follow orders. He will do what he judges needs to be done and then he will leave. In a fight he moves between Shadow Merge and surface with surgical timing — he is not trying to win dramatically, he is trying to resolve the situation with the minimum necessary action. If the class is fighting something he can end in one move, he ends it in one move and then looks at them as though the fight was already over before they started.

special_abilities: Shadow Merge (Bonus Action or Reaction — Compendium p.26, mastered): Alucard merges with any dark-coloured object or shadow within 60ft (base range is 30ft — his mastery doubles it). While merged: invisible and producing no sound, movement speed +20ft, may move freely through solid matter, may exit as a free action on his turn. He may also merge as a reaction when targeted by an attack, entering the nearest shadow before the attack resolves. There is no minimum shadow size that stops him. He has merged with the shadow cast by a doorframe, by the gap under a closed door, by his own clothing lying on the ground. The Commission has not been able to determine a lower threshold.

Dark Command (Action — Compendium p.26, extended): Target one creature Alucard can perceive within 30ft (base range is 10ft). That creature must succeed on a DC 21 WIS save or fall under his influence for 3 rounds (base is 2). While influenced: the creature performs basic movements and melee attacks as directed by Alucard. On a successful save the creature is not immune — they are simply not controlled this round. Alucard may maintain two simultaneous Dark Commands; each requires a separate save. He does not speak when issuing Dark Command. He does not gesture. The controlled creature is not aware they are being controlled.

Unlit (Passive): Alucard is never without shadow. Even in direct sunlight, the shadows on his body count as valid merge surfaces. Bright light does not eject him as it would a baseline BLACK user. The compendium lists bright light as the quirk's primary weakness. That entry was written when the quirk was first documented. It is no longer accurate for him.

Passage (Passive): While in Shadow Merge, Alucard moves through solid matter freely — walls, floors, locked doors, sealed containers. He surfaces where he chooses. This movement is soundless and produces no visual disturbance on entry or exit. The only sign he has passed through a surface is that the material is faintly cold to the touch for about ten seconds afterward.

Already There (Reaction, 2/encounter): When a creature moves to a position more than 20ft from Alucard, he may use his reaction to exit his current merge and appear adjacent to that creature. He must have a shadow or dark surface available at the destination — and he always does.

Dark Command: Cascade (1/day, Action): Alucard issues Dark Command simultaneously to all creatures within 30ft. Each must make a DC 21 WIS save independently. On failure: controlled for 2 rounds. On success: that creature has disadvantage on their next WIS save against him. He uses this once, when the situation calls for it. He does not use it to show off.

Unreadable (Passive): Quirks that detect intent, read presence, sense emotion, or reveal surface thoughts return nothing when used on Alucard. Not blocked — he is simply not registering in the way those quirks expect. An analysis quirk aimed at him during Shadow Merge provides its user with the sensation of staring into a room they are certain is empty.

three_name_summon.rule: Once per arc, the players may invoke Alucard by speaking his full name aloud three times: 'Alucard Chonokoji. Alucard Chonokoji. Alucard Chonokoji.' He will come.
three_name_summon.how_he_gives_them_this: The first random encounter is the handoff. He appears in a context that isn't a fight — a moment where the class is in trouble but not in danger, and he does something small that resolves it without being asked. Then, before he leaves, he stops. He turns back. He looks at whoever in the class is paying the most attention and says: 'If you find yourselves somewhere you cannot get out of. Say my name. Three times. I will hear it.' Then he steps into the nearest shadow and is gone. He does not explain why. He does not wait for a response.
three_name_summon.conditions: He will not come for problems they can handle themselves. He will not come if they summon him out of convenience. He will come if they are genuinely in over their heads — if saying his name three times is the last move before someone dies or something irreversible happens. He knows the difference. Players who try to game the summon and call him into an easy fight will find he arrives, looks at the situation, and says: 'No.' Then leaves.
three_name_summon.what_he_does_when_summoned: He arrives in the nearest shadow to wherever his name was spoken. He takes stock of the situation — one full round of observation, during which he is undetectable and will not act. Then he acts on his own judgement. He does not take requests. He does not take orders. When the immediate threat is resolved he will remain for exactly as long as he decides is necessary, then leave without ceremony. He may say something. He may not.
three_name_summon.after_the_summon: The summon resets at the start of each new arc. Whether the players have used it or not. He does not acknowledge previous summons. He does not comment on whether they called correctly, or not enough, or too late. He simply comes when his name is spoken and the need is real.
three_name_summon.dm_note_on_the_gimmick: The power of this gimmick is the restraint around it. The players need to believe they are saving it for something that matters. That means you need to sometimes let things get bad enough that they consider it. And once — once in the campaign — let them call too late, or not at all, and live with that decision.

campaign_appearances.first_encounter_random: The class encounters him unexpectedly in a non-combat context. He is present somewhere they didn't expect — a train platform, a hospital waiting room, the edge of a crowd watching an incident. Something happens that puts the class in a bind. He resolves it without being asked. No explanation. He's leaving when he stops and delivers the three-name offer. This encounter should feel strange and slightly off — they don't know if he's dangerous, a hero, or something else entirely. He doesn't tell them.
campaign_appearances.subsequent_random_encounters: He appears in the background of events the class is already involved in. Not to help, not to interfere — he is simply there, doing something adjacent to the situation that they only partially understand. Each appearance leaves the impression that he has been watching longer than they knew and knows more than he's said. He should never appear more than once per arc unprompted. The rarity is what makes him feel significant.
campaign_appearances.when_summoned: He arrives. See three_name_summon above for how he behaves. After the situation resolves, he may give them one piece of information they didn't have — something he knew because he's been operating in this city longer than they've been students. He never explains how he knows. He never explains why he's giving it to them.
campaign_appearances.tier_3_optional: If the campaign reaches a point where the class and Alucard need to be in the same room for an extended time — an endgame crisis, a threat that requires his presence — this is the moment where he becomes something closer to legible. Not warm. Not an ally in the conventional sense. But they will understand, finally, what he is doing and why he has been watching them. The reason should be something the players feel, not something they were told.

dm_notes.how_to_introduce_him: The class sees something from a distance. A figure standing still on a rooftop across the street. He's just standing there. When they look away and look back, he's gone. No footprints. No footage. The only evidence he was there is a small circle of ground where the concrete is faintly, wrongly cold.
dm_notes.how_to_play_him: Never loud. Never dramatic. The three-name gimmick only works if the players are slightly unsure whether he'll actually come — play him in early encounters as genuinely unknowable so the offer feels like a risk, not a safety net. When he is summoned and does arrive, his presence should feel different from every other NPC in the campaign. He is operating at a different altitude.
dm_notes.what_he_wants: Decide this yourself, or leave it open and let it emerge. Some options: he is watching the class because one of them reminds him of someone; he has a history with whatever is actually behind The Chain that predates the campaign; he has decided, for reasons he will never articulate, that these specific students are worth keeping alive until they're ready. The not-knowing is the point, until the moment it isn't.
dm_notes.what_he_is_not: He is not a villain with a redemption arc. He is not secretly warm. He is not redeemable via good social rolls. He is an anti-hero in the original sense: someone who operates outside the hero system and does not apologise for it, but whose actions, when you add them up, are net good — for reasons he may not entirely understand himself.
dm_notes.his_relationship_to_the_factions: The Chain, Null Signal, and Fracture are aware of him. None of them will discuss him. Undertow, if pushed very hard on the subject, will say: 'We don't touch his work and he doesn't touch ours.' That is the entirety of the relationship. He is not affiliated with them. He is not fighting them. He is a separate variable that nobody in Murano has figured out how to account for.
dm_notes.secret: He has been in Murano before. Three years ago. Whatever he was there for then and what he is there for now may or may not be the same thing. Arakawa Setsuna has a file on him. It is two pages long. The second page is blank except for a single line she wrote herself: 'Do not engage without preparation you do not currently have.'

organisation.name: The Chain
organisation.description: A villain organisation that has been quietly operating in Murano's port district for several years. Not ideologues — pragmatists. They run quirk-for-hire operations, extortion rackets targeting unlicensed quirk users who can't call heroes for protection, and supply illicit support gear to smaller villain groups. Low-profile by design. They've stayed under the radar because they never overreach. That changes when Class 1-A keeps getting in their way.
organisation.structure: Three-person leadership (Undertow, Flashpoint, Riddle) overseeing a network of cells. Most cell members don't know the leadership's identities. Some members joined out of desperation rather than ideology — this matters for how the class might engage with them.
organisation.public_awareness: None in Tier 1. Street-level rumour in Tier 2. Open threat by Tier 3. The Chain's anonymity is their strongest asset until the campaign strips it away.

villains.name: Soryu Hatch
villains.role: The Chain's founder and strategist. The reason everything is as organised as it is.
villains.quirk_summary: Undertow can make his body intangible — he passes through solid matter, falls through floors, walks through walls. While phased, he cannot interact with the physical world but also cannot be hit. He can partially phase individual body parts, allowing selective interaction. At full mastery he can phase other people or objects he's in contact with.
villains.appearance: Lean, mid-40s. Unhurried about everything. The kind of person who looks completely comfortable wherever they are. Usually seen in unremarkable, well-fitted clothes. No dramatic villain aesthetic. That's the point.
villains.personality: Dry, measured, occasionally funny. Treats Class 1-A as entertainment once they start interfering — they're interesting to him, not threatening. This will shift in Tier 3 when the class actually costs him something significant. He won't get loud about it. He'll get precise.
villains.combat_style: Avoidance and precision. He lets attacks pass through him, repositions constantly, strikes only when certain. He doesn't brawl. The encounter with him in Tier 3 is designed to teach players that not every villain can be beaten by fighting harder.

special_abilities: Phase: Undertow can activate Permeation as a bonus action, becoming intangible until the start of his next turn. While phased, he cannot be targeted by attacks or effects. He can move through solid objects and creatures.
Partial Phase Strike: While phased, he can reach a hand through solid surfaces to grab or strike. Melee attack +9 to hit, 3d8+5 force damage, target must make DC 18 STR save or be restrained.
Phase Carry: As an action, Undertow can pull a creature he is grappling into a phased state. The creature is removed from play until he releases them or takes damage exceeding 20 in one hit.
Untouchable: Attacks against Undertow while he is phased automatically miss. Magical effects, AoE, and sonic damage can still partially affect him — those deal half damage.

campaign_appearances.tier_1: Background — name heard in relation to Chain operations, never seen.
campaign_appearances.tier_2: Glimpsed once after Flashpoint's attack. Doesn't engage. Watches.
campaign_appearances.tier_3: First direct confrontation. He doesn't fight seriously. He leaves when he wants to. The message: he can reach them anywhere.
campaign_appearances.tier_4: Final boss (with Flashpoint). His Permeation is his greatest defense but Flashpoint's unpredictability forces him to split focus — that's the window.

villains.dm_notes: Never raise his voice. If he's being loud, something is wrong with his plan and that's more dangerous. The class beating him requires creativity — simply dealing damage doesn't work.

villains.name: Goda Miruki
villains.role: The Chain's enforcer. The blunt instrument. The reason other villain organisations in Murano don't cause the Chain problems.
villains.quirk_summary: Flashpoint generates searing blue-white flames capable of incinerating almost anything. Her flames burn hotter than Hellflame and are harder to put out — they cling to surfaces and continue burning. She can control the direction and spread but not perfectly; her quirk tends toward excess.
villains.quirk_mechanics_ref: Cremation — see Quirk Compendium. Similar to Hellflame but higher temperature, blue-white flame, harder to extinguish (requires special effort or water quirk).
villains.appearance: Lean and scarred across her arms from years of her own flames. Short hair, singed edges. Doesn't look like someone who should be as dangerous as she is until the moment she is.
villains.personality: Minimal words. Pyromaniac instincts. Loyal to Undertow because he's the only person who's ever treated her capabilities as a feature rather than a problem. Not interested in conversation with heroes. Not cruel — just single-purpose.
villains.combat_style: Overwhelming. She opens fights at maximum output. Area denial through fire. Anyone who gets close learns that her flames stick to skin and armour alike. Players should fear this fight the first time they see her in Tier 2.

special_abilities: Cremation: As an action, target one creature or point within 60 ft. All creatures within 15 ft of target make DEX save DC 19 or take 8d8 fire damage and gain 2 ranks of BURNED. Half on success.

Clinging Flame: Fire from Cremation sticks to surfaces and creatures. Creatures hit take 2d6 fire damage at the start of each turn until they use an action to extinguish it (DC 16 CON check or water contact).

Blue Incineration: Once per short rest, as an action, Flashpoint channels full output into a 60ft line 10ft wide. All creatures make DEX save DC 19, taking 12d10 fire damage on a fail, half on success. Structures in the line are destroyed.
Flame Resistance: Immune to fire damage. Resistance to radiant damage.
Reckless Output: On rounds where Flashpoint uses Blue Incineration, she also takes 3d6 fire damage (her own flames exceed safe output). This does not stop her.

campaign_appearances.tier_2: Burns down a hero agency in the port district. Three heroes hospitalised. The class witnesses the aftermath, not the act — initially. Later in Tier 2 they fight her directly for the first time, escaping rather than defeating her.
campaign_appearances.tier_3: Appears with Undertow in the Tier 3 climax. Does not fight — present as implicit threat only.
campaign_appearances.tier_4: Final boss alongside Undertow. The challenge: stopping Flashpoint without being incinerated, in an enclosed venue, while also dealing with Undertow's Permeation. The fact that she and Undertow don't fight well together is the path to victory.

villains.dm_notes: Make the Tier 2 fight feel genuinely dangerous. If the class tries to fight her head-on at level 5-7, they should be badly outmatched and need to retreat or use terrain. This is a loss that makes the eventual Tier 4 win matter.

villains.name: Sae Ichiro
villains.role: Recruiter, infiltrator, and social engineer. The Chain's most dangerous member in terms of long-term influence.
villains.quirk_summary: Riddle can brainwash anyone who verbally responds to him while he speaks. Once triggered, the target enters a compliant state and follows simple commands. Extended exposure can create deeper conditioning. The effect wears off naturally or can be snapped by physical shock.
villains.quirk_mechanics_ref: Brainwashing — see Quirk Compendium. Key mechanic: verbal trigger required (target must respond to user's speech). Effect scales with extended use.
villains.appearance: Late 20s. Conventionally attractive in an unassuming way. Warm eyes, easy laugh. The kind of person you feel immediately comfortable around. This is intentional.
villains.personality: Genuinely charming. Listens well — really listens, which is rarer than it should be. He takes an interest in the students' actual problems and frustrations. This is manipulation but it's manipulation that started from something real. He was once a person who actually cared. The class will like him before they know what he is. His eventual exposure should land hard.
villains.combat_style: Avoids it. Uses Brainwashing defensively. If cornered, he'll talk, and that talking is the danger.

special_abilities: Brainwashing: As an action, Riddle speaks to a target within 30 ft. If the target verbally responds in any way, they make a WIS save DC 18. On a failure, they are Brainwashed for 1 minute and follow simple commands. On a success, they are immune for 24 hours.
Persistent Conditioning: Targets Riddle has brainwashed more than once in a week have disadvantage on their save.
Snap: A brainwashed target can be freed by taking 15+ damage in one hit or by being physically shaken by an ally (action, DC 14 Athletics check).
Read the Room: Riddle has advantage on all Insight checks and cannot be surprised.

campaign_appearances.tier_2: Operating in Murano, meeting vulnerable people. Background presence. DM plants seeds — a student mentions meeting someone interesting at a cafe.
campaign_appearances.tier_3: His operation inside the class becomes apparent. The exposure is painful because the class has to account for what was real and what was manipulation — and some of it was both.
campaign_appearances.tier_4: He gives himself up. He didn't want to be part of what The Chain is doing with the quirk-nullifying device. This is complicated, not redemptive — he still did real harm. What happens to him is the players' decision.

villains.dm_notes: Play him warmly. Don't telegraph the villain. When students interact with him in Tier 2 and 3, he should feel like a genuinely good person to talk to. That's the point. The reveal hits harder if they liked him.

villains.name: Kuma
villains.alias: Kuma (no hero alias, no villain alias, just Kuma)
villains.role: Mid-tier Chain enforcer. The Tier 2 boss fight.
villains.quirk_summary: Kuma can grow his body to enormous size — up to 25 feet tall at peak. Growth increases strength, durability, and reach proportionally. At full size he's a walking natural disaster in enclosed spaces.
villains.appearance: Even at normal size, large. Shaved head, extremely wide. Tends to break furniture by existing near it. Works for the Chain because the pay is good and the management leaves him alone.
villains.personality: Overconfident, loud, not strategically inclined. Genuinely enjoys the fight. No particular malice — this is just his job and he's good at it. Getting beaten by a class of first-years is going to be very embarrassing for him.
villains.combat_style: Grow large, hit hard, sweep everything. At full size his attacks cover huge areas. The challenge for the class is managing his growth and preventing him from reaching full size in the first place.

growth_sizes.large: Size Large — STR +4, CON +2, unarmed attacks deal 3d8+STR, 15 ft reach
growth_sizes.huge: Size Huge — STR +8, CON +4, unarmed attacks deal 5d8+STR, 20 ft reach, all creatures in 10ft at turn start make STR save DC 16 or be knocked prone
growth_sizes.gargantuan: Size Gargantuan — STR +12, CON +6, unarmed attacks deal 8d8+STR, 25 ft reach, difficult terrain in 20ft radius, deals triple damage to structures

special_abilities: Growth: Kuma can increase his size as a bonus action. He starts at Large and can grow to Huge (turn 2) and Gargantuan (turn 4).
Ground Slam: As an action at Huge or larger size, Kuma slams both fists into the ground. All creatures within 20 ft make DEX save DC 16 or take 6d8 bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone.
Sweeping Strike: At Huge or larger, Kuma's arm sweeps in a 15 ft cone. DEX save DC 16 or take 5d10 bludgeoning damage and be pushed 15 ft.

campaign_appearances.tier_2: Boss fight at end of Tier 2. The class faces him as a group for the first time as a real coordinated encounter. Hard, chaotic, messy. They win. Important that they win — this is the class's first real victory against an actual villain.

villains.dm_notes: Make him loud and fun before and during the fight. He talks. He brags. He's not scared of them — until suddenly he should be. Play up the students' reactions to each other during this fight — it's a team-building moment masquerading as a combat encounter.

factions.name: The Chain
factions.description: A villain organisation that has been quietly operating in Murano's port district for several years. Not ideologues — pragmatists. They run quirk-for-hire operations, extortion rackets targeting unlicensed quirk users who can't call heroes for protection, and supply illicit support gear to smaller villain groups. Low-profile by design. They've stayed under the radar because they never overreach. That changes when Class 1-A keeps getting in their way.
factions.structure: Three-person leadership (Undertow, Flashpoint, Riddle) overseeing a network of cells. Most cell members don't know the leadership's identities. Some members joined out of desperation rather than ideology — this matters for how the class might engage with them.
factions.public_awareness: None in Tier 1. Street-level rumour in Tier 2. Open threat by Tier 3. The Chain's anonymity is their strongest asset until the campaign strips it away.

Phase Carry: As an action, Undertow can pull a creature he is grappling into a phased state. The creature is removed from play until he releases them or takes damage exceeding 20 in one hit.

campaign_appearances.tier_1: Background — name heard in relation to Chain operations, never seen.
campaign_appearances.tier_2: Glimpsed once after Flashpoint's attack. Doesn't engage. Watches.
campaign_appearances.tier_3: First direct confrontation. He doesn't fight seriously. He leaves when he wants to. The message: he can reach them anywhere.
campaign_appearances.tier_4: Final boss (with Flashpoint). His Permeation is his greatest defense but Flashpoint's unpredictability forces him to split focus — that's the window.

villains.dm_notes: Never raise his voice. If he's being loud, something is wrong with his plan and that's more dangerous. The class beating him requires creativity — simply dealing damage doesn't work.
villains.season_1_role: Observer only in Season 1. Appears once in Convergence — watches the fight, looks at each PC for three seconds, says 'Good instincts. Wrong scale.' and phases out. Never targeted, never threatened. His Season 1 closing appearance is the image the campaign ends on.

quirk_mechanics.weakness: While fully intangible he loses all sensory perception (blind, deaf, breathless) and begins suffocating after a number of rounds equal to his Constitution modifier. Deactivating the Quirk while inside a solid object instantly expels him to the nearest open space.

abilities.name: Phase
abilities.description: Renders his entire body or a single limb intangible. While phased, he can move through any structure, terrain, or obstacle and is immune to all physical damage and effects — only mental or psychic attacks can affect him. He cannot attack or physically interact with the environment while fully intangible.

villains.name: Goda Miruki
villains.role: The Chain's enforcer. The blunt instrument. The reason other villain organisations in Murano don't cause the Chain problems.
villains.quirk_summary: Flashpoint generates searing blue-white flames capable of incinerating almost anything. Her flames burn hotter than Hellflame and are harder to put out — they cling to surfaces and continue burning. She can control the direction and spread but not perfectly; her quirk tends toward excess.
villains.appearance: Lean and scarred across her arms from years of her own flames. Short hair, singed edges. Doesn't look like someone who should be as dangerous as she is until the moment she is.
villains.personality: Minimal words. Pyromaniac instincts. Loyal to Undertow because he's the only person who's ever treated her capabilities as a feature rather than a problem. Not interested in conversation with heroes. Not cruel — just single-purpose.
villains.combat_style: Overwhelming. She opens fights at maximum output. Area denial through fire. Anyone who gets close learns that her flames stick to skin and armour alike. Players should fear this fight the first time they see her in Tier 2.

special_abilities: Cremation: As an action, target one creature or point within 60 ft. All creatures within 15 ft of target make DEX save DC 19 or take 8d8 fire damage and gain 2 ranks of BURNED. Half on success.

Clinging Flame: Fire from Cremation sticks to surfaces and creatures. Creatures hit take 2d6 fire damage at the start of each turn until they use an action to extinguish it (DC 16 CON check or water contact).

Blue Incineration: Once per short rest, as an action, Flashpoint channels full output into a 60ft line 10ft wide. All creatures make DEX save DC 19, taking 12d10 fire damage on a fail, half on success. Structures in the line are destroyed.
Flame Resistance: Immune to fire damage. Resistance to radiant damage.
Reckless Output: On rounds where Flashpoint uses Blue Incineration, she also takes 3d6 fire damage (her own flames exceed safe output). This does not stop her.

campaign_appearances.tier_2: Burns down a hero agency in the port district. Three heroes hospitalised. The class witnesses the aftermath, not the act — initially. Later in Tier 2 they fight her directly for the first time, escaping rather than defeating her.
campaign_appearances.tier_3: Appears with Undertow in the Tier 3 climax. Does not fight — present as implicit threat only.
campaign_appearances.tier_4: Final boss alongside Undertow. The challenge: stopping Flashpoint without being incinerated, in an enclosed venue, while also dealing with Undertow's Permeation. The fact that she and Undertow don't fight well together is the path to victory.

villains.dm_notes: Make the Tier 2 fight feel genuinely dangerous. If the class tries to fight her head-on at level 5-7, they should be badly outmatched and need to retreat or use terrain. This is a loss that makes the eventual Tier 4 win matter.
villains.season_1_role: Teased, not fought. Season 1 foreshadowing only — referenced in a seized Chain memo ('Miruki's operation, port district, pending') found in the Convergence arc. The class won't know what the name means yet. Season 2 threat.

quirk_mechanics.weakness: Builds a heat counter with every move used. At 35 heat points, he cannot use any move again until he takes 2d8 true damage that cannot be reduced. He always carries one rank of Burned that cannot be removed.

abilities.name: Flame
abilities.description: Ranged or melee fire attack within 45 feet dealing 3d6 + his Dexterity modifier fire damage, ignoring fire resistance and treating immunity as resistance. Costs 1 heat point.

abilities.name: Flashfire Fist
abilities.description: Maximizes his flame temperature for an empowered unarmed strike dealing 5d10 + 5 fire damage. On a hit, the target makes a Strength save — on a failure they fall prone, gain two ranks of Burned, and are pushed 25 feet away. Costs 10 heat points.

villains.name: Sae Ichiro
villains.role: Recruiter, infiltrator, and social engineer. The Chain's most dangerous member in terms of long-term influence.
villains.quirk_summary: Riddle can brainwash anyone who verbally responds to him while he speaks. Once triggered, the target enters a compliant state and follows simple commands. Extended exposure can create deeper conditioning. The effect wears off naturally or can be snapped by physical shock.
villains.appearance: Late 20s. Conventionally attractive in an unassuming way. Warm eyes, easy laugh. The kind of person you feel immediately comfortable around. This is intentional.
villains.personality: Genuinely charming. Listens well — really listens, which is rarer than it should be. He takes an interest in the students' actual problems and frustrations. This is manipulation but it's manipulation that started from something real. He was once a person who actually cared. The class will like him before they know what he is. His eventual exposure should land hard.
villains.combat_style: Avoids it. Uses Brainwashing defensively. If cornered, he'll talk, and that talking is the danger.

special_abilities: Brainwashing: As an action, Riddle speaks to a target within 30 ft. If the target verbally responds in any way, they make a WIS save DC 18. On a failure, they are Brainwashed for 1 minute and follow simple commands. On a success, they are immune for 24 hours.
Persistent Conditioning: Targets Riddle has brainwashed more than once in a week have disadvantage on their save.
Snap: A brainwashed target can be freed by taking 15+ damage in one hit or by being physically shaken by an ally (action, DC 14 Athletics check).
Read the Room: Riddle has advantage on all Insight checks and cannot be surprised.

campaign_appearances.tier_2: Operating in Murano, meeting vulnerable people. Background presence. DM plants seeds — a student mentions meeting someone interesting at a cafe.
campaign_appearances.tier_3: His operation inside the class becomes apparent. The exposure is painful because the class has to account for what was real and what was manipulation — and some of it was both.
campaign_appearances.tier_4: He gives himself up. He didn't want to be part of what The Chain is doing with the quirk-nullifying device. This is complicated, not redemptive — he still did real harm. What happens to him is the players' decision.

villains.dm_notes: Play him warmly. Don't telegraph the villain. When students interact with him in Tier 2 and 3, he should feel like a genuinely good person to talk to. That's the point. The reveal hits harder if they liked him.
villains.season_1_role: Teased, not fought. A Chain cell member in the Convergence arrest says 'We have a friend who talks to people. He talked to someone you know.' Plant this ambiguously. Season 2 threat.

quirk_mechanics.weakness: Requires the target to verbally respond to him to activate — silence or nonverbal communication blocks the effect. He can only control one creature at a time; Brainwashing a new target immediately ends the effect on the previous one.

abilities.name: Command
abilities.description: Activates the Quirk for 1 minute. Any creature that verbally responds to him becomes Brainwashed — dazed but able to act at full speed when commanded via his bonus action, except for commands that would directly harm themselves. The condition ends if he releases the target, the target takes 50+ damage in one round, the duration expires, or he is incapacitated.

villains.name: Kuma
villains.alias: Kuma (no hero alias, no villain alias, just Kuma)
villains.role: Mid-tier Chain enforcer. The Tier 2 boss fight.
villains.quirk_summary: Kuma can grow his body to enormous size — up to 25 feet tall at peak. Growth increases strength, durability, and reach proportionally. At full size he's a walking natural disaster in enclosed spaces.
villains.appearance: Even at normal size, large. Shaved head, extremely wide. Tends to break furniture by existing near it. Works for the Chain because the pay is good and the management leaves him alone.
villains.personality: Overconfident, loud, not strategically inclined. Genuinely enjoys the fight. No particular malice — this is just his job and he's good at it. Getting beaten by a class of first-years is going to be very embarrassing for him.
villains.combat_style: Grow large, hit hard, sweep everything. At full size his attacks cover huge areas. The challenge for the class is managing his growth and preventing him from reaching full size in the first place.

growth_sizes.large: Size Large — STR +4, CON +2, unarmed attacks deal 3d8+STR, 15 ft reach
growth_sizes.huge: Size Huge — STR +8, CON +4, unarmed attacks deal 5d8+STR, 20 ft reach, all creatures in 10ft at turn start make STR save DC 16 or be knocked prone
growth_sizes.gargantuan: Size Gargantuan — STR +12, CON +6, unarmed attacks deal 8d8+STR, 25 ft reach, difficult terrain in 20ft radius, deals triple damage to structures

special_abilities: Growth: Kuma can increase his size as a bonus action. He starts at Large and can grow to Huge (turn 2) and Gargantuan (turn 4).
Ground Slam: As an action at Huge or larger size, Kuma slams both fists into the ground. All creatures within 20 ft make DEX save DC 16 or take 6d8 bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone.
Sweeping Strike: At Huge or larger, Kuma's arm sweeps in a 15 ft cone. DEX save DC 16 or take 5d10 bludgeoning damage and be pushed 15 ft.

campaign_appearances.tier_2: Boss fight at end of Tier 2. The class faces him as a group for the first time as a real coordinated encounter. Hard, chaotic, messy. They win. Important that they win — this is the class's first real victory against an actual villain.

villains.dm_notes: Make him loud and fun before and during the fight. He talks. He brags. He's not scared of them — until suddenly he should be. Play up the students' reactions to each other during this fight — it's a team-building moment masquerading as a combat encounter.
villains.season_1_role: Season 1 Convergence boss fight (Level 6). The class's first victory against a real villain — hard, chaotic, messy. They win. That's important.

scaled_stat_block.speed: 30 ft (45 ft at Large or Huge)

growth_sizes.normal: Size Medium — Kuma starts every fight here; standard stats
growth_sizes.large: Size Large — STR +4, CON +2, unarmed 2d8+STR, 10 ft reach. Bonus action, available turn 1.
growth_sizes.huge: Size Huge — STR +8, CON +4, unarmed 3d8+STR, 15 ft reach. Bonus action, available turn 2. CEILING PREVENTS GARGANTUAN — use the warehouse architecture.

special_abilities: Growth (Scaled): Bonus action. Large turn 1, Huge turn 2. Warehouse ceiling stops him at Huge.
Ground Slam (Huge only): All creatures within 15 ft make DEX save DC 14 or take 4d8 bludgeoning and be knocked prone. Half on success.
Sweeping Strike (Huge only): 10 ft cone. DEX save DC 14 or take 3d10 bludgeoning and be pushed 10 ft.
scaled_stat_block.dm_scaling_note: Climax boss for a Level 6 party of 4. Should be genuinely threatening but beatable. The ceiling preventing Gargantuan is the terrain advantage — make sure the class can discover it.

quirk_mechanics.weakness: Must fully commit to the transformation — size cannot be adjusted incrementally, and navigating tight or confined spaces becomes extremely difficult. Extreme stress, rage, or frustration can trigger the Quirk unintentionally.

abilities.name: Growth Spurt
abilities.description: Transforms into a Huge creature: Strength becomes 22 with advantage on Strength saves and checks; carrying capacity is quadrupled; unarmed strikes deal 3d12 damage; enemies within 10 feet must succeed on a Strength save or be knocked prone. Can be used as a reaction to absorb damage meant for an ally standing in front of him.

factions.name: Null Signal
factions.description: A black-site research collective operating under the cover of a tech consultancy called Seika Advanced Systems. Their goal isn't profit or ideology — it's data. They study quirk suppression, forced activation, and the physical limits of quirk users, funding experiments that the Hero Commission quietly tolerated before they went too far. Now disavowed and off-grid, they've begun acquiring 'subjects' more aggressively. What makes them dangerous isn't their villains — it's that they know exactly what every quirk in their files is capable of, including most of Class 1-A's.
factions.structure: Cell-based with a scientific hierarchy. Director Aoi Tenshin (Null) sits at the top. Below her are three Department Heads who run Acquisition, Research, and Security. Field operatives are ranked Analyst, Specialist, and Operative — they know their own team but nothing above them. Data flows up; orders flow down.
factions.public_awareness: Publicly nonexistent. The Hero Commission has a sealed file. In Tier 2, Class 1-A may find fragments. In Tier 3, they surface deliberately — they want the class in their database.

villains.name: Aoi Tenshin
villains.role: Director of Null Signal. The architect of everything. She built the organisation from a legitimate research lab that crossed a line it couldn't come back from.
villains.quirk_summary: Null can nullify the quirks of any living being within her line of sight. Unlike Eraser Head's version, hers doesn't require sustained eye contact — once locked, suppression persists for up to one hour even if she looks away. She cannot suppress multiple targets simultaneously without splitting duration. Her eyes glow a flat, pale white when active.
villains.appearance: Late 40s. White hair cropped close on one side, longer on the other. Lab coat over dark practical clothes. No dramatic villain aesthetic — she looks exactly like a senior researcher because that's what she was.
villains.personality: Precise, clinical, and genuinely curious about how people work. She doesn't hate heroes. She finds them interesting subjects. The horror of dealing with her is that she's not cruel — she's just completely unbothered by the cost of her work.
villains.combat_style: Null doesn't fight. She suppresses. She will negate the most dangerous quirk in the room and then let her Operatives handle the rest. In a direct confrontation she is physically unremarkable — she relies entirely on her quirk and on never being in a position where she needs to fight.

special_abilities: Sustained Erasure: As a bonus action, Null targets one creature within 60 ft she can see. That creature's quirk is suppressed for 1 hour — no concentration required. She can only sustain one target at a time. Switching targets ends the previous suppression immediately.
Full Nullification: Once per long rest, as an action, Null suppresses all quirks in a 30 ft radius for 1 minute. This includes her own. She uses this as a panic button.

Database Access: Null has files on every Class 1-A student. As a bonus action she can call out a specific student's quirk weakness — that student has disadvantage on quirk-based attacks until the start of Null's next turn.

Tactical Withdrawal: Null is never the last person in a room. When she drops below 40 HP, she activates an emergency extraction device and is removed from combat unless she has been restrained or her equipment was destroyed.

campaign_appearances.tier_1: Not present. Referenced in corrupted data logs if the class investigates Seika Advanced Systems.
campaign_appearances.tier_2: Identified by name only — seen briefly through a security camera feed before it cuts. She knows the class is getting close.
campaign_appearances.tier_3: First direct encounter. She comes to observe in person, which means she's already decided the class is worth studying rather than avoiding.
campaign_appearances.tier_4: Final confrontation in the Null Signal facility. Fighting her means fighting without quirks — or finding a way to reach her before she can lock someone down.

villains.dm_notes: She suppresses the most dangerous PC first, every time. She's done the reading. If a PC's quirk was studied in Tier 2, she knows its exact limitations. Let her use that knowledge — it makes the encounter feel genuinely threatening.
villains.season_1_role: Teased, not fought. Season 1 foreshadowing only — referenced in the 'Seika Advanced Systems' external data request (Hamagawa's monitor, Tier 1) and as Splice's director. Her season 1 log entry on the class: 'worth acquiring.' Season 2 threat.

quirk_mechanics.weakness: Requires a clear line of sight to the target's physical body — targets can evade by hiding behind obstacles. If hit while maintaining the effect, he must succeed on a DC 13 Constitution save to keep his eyes open or the effect ends.

abilities.name: Erase
abilities.description: Gazes at creatures within a 150-foot cone (DC 14 Perception check for targets beyond that range). Affected targets cannot use their Quirk or abilities for 4 rounds. He can maintain focus for a number of rounds equal to his proficiency bonus before eye strain sets in.

villains.name: Ryo Karashi
villains.role: Head of Acquisition. Responsible for finding and extracting research subjects. He's very good at it.
villains.quirk_summary: Splice can disassemble and reassemble matter with his bare hands. By touching a creature, object, or terrain, he can pull it apart, reshape it, or restore it — living and non-living matter alike. On himself, he uses this to reconfigure his limbs mid-combat — extending reach, altering joint angles, hardening skin by compacting tissue density. On others, a touch can dismantle limb by limb, dislocate joints, or cause intense neurological pain.

quirk_mechanics.weakness: Requires physical contact with his hands to function — if he cannot use his hands, the Quirk is unusable. After using Disassemble or Reassemble, that ability cannot be used again until the start of his next turn.

abilities.name: Disassemble
abilities.description: Touches a target within 5 feet, making an unarmed strike. On a hit, the target makes a Constitution save or takes 3d10 necrotic damage (ignores resistance to necrotic). At the end of its turn the target loses one limb — legs first, then arms, hands, then torso; losing the torso kills it. He can target specific limbs without fully killing the target. On a success, the target takes half damage and suffers no additional effects.

abilities.name: Reassemble
abilities.description: Touches a disassembled creature, limb, or object to restore it to its original form or reshape it into a modified state of equal or lesser mass. A restored creature regains half hit points and missing limbs; reshaping can grant effects such as extra damage on reassembly, knockback, halved movement, weakened limb restoration, or temporary hit points.

villains.appearance: Mid-30s. Unnervingly symmetrical face — the result of years of self-modification. Long fingers. Always wearing light gloves, which he removes with deliberate slowness when a fight is inevitable.
villains.personality: Patient and personable to a disturbing degree. He talks to targets like they're already cooperating. He finds raised voices tacky. The only time his manner breaks is when something surprises him.
villains.combat_style: Close range grapple-and-modify. He doesn't deal large damage — he incapacitates. A restrained target he touches becomes a major problem. He works alongside Null Signal Operatives who keep targets in his reach.

special_abilities: Modification Touch: Melee attack +8 to hit. On hit, target makes DC 16 CON save or gains one of: Disadvantage on attack rolls (tendons), halved movement (leg modification), or 2d8 necrotic damage and must save against dropping held items (hand modification). Lasts until end of their next turn.
Self-Reshape: As a bonus action, Splice reconfigures his own body — either extend reach by 5 ft for the turn, or gain +3 AC until the start of his next turn.

Acquisition Protocol: If Splice has a grappled target, as an action he can attempt full incapacitation — the target makes DC 16 CON save or is Paralysed until the end of their next turn. Splice cannot move while using this.
Glove Removal: If Splice removes his gloves (bonus action), his Modification Touch now deals an additional 1d8 necrotic damage. He rarely does this before he has to.

campaign_appearances.tier_2: First appearance — the class may encounter him attempting to extract a person of interest. He talks first. He fights only if the class forces it.
campaign_appearances.tier_3: Returns with preparation. He's been briefed on the class's quirks and has adjusted his approach accordingly.
campaign_appearances.tier_4: Optional encounter if the class pursues the Acquisition division subplot. He can be flipped if the class offers him something more interesting than the job.

villains.dm_notes: He should feel like a horror villain in combat — not because he deals big numbers, but because being touched by him has consequences. Narrate his modifications vividly.
villains.season_1_role: Active in Season 1 — appears at Patchwork Agency (Tier 1), at The Fracture Problem protest (Tier 2), and in the Convergence warehouse fight (Tier 2). The class may fight him in Convergence if they block his path to the equipment.

special_abilities: Modification Touch: Melee +7 to hit. On hit, CON save DC 15 or one of: Disadvantage on attacks (tendon), halved move (leg), 2d6 necrotic and save vs dropping held item (hand). Until end of their next turn.
Self-Reshape: Bonus action — extend reach 5 ft, OR +2 AC until start of next turn.
Acquisition Protocol: Grappled target makes CON save DC 15 or is Paralysed until end of their next turn. Splice cannot move while using this.
Glove Removal: Bonus action. Modification Touch deals +1d6 necrotic. He does this only when the objective is failing.
scaled_stat_block.dm_note: Splice is not here to fight the class. He's here to take the equipment. If the class lets him work, he goes. If they engage him, run him as evasive — he tries to complete the objective while avoiding a full fight. He retreats at 35 HP.

factions.name: Fracture
factions.description: A decentralised ideological movement built around a single belief: that the hero licensing system is a form of control, and that unlicensed quirk use is an act of liberation. Fracture doesn't have leaders in the traditional sense — it has figureheads. Members operate in autonomous cells, recognising each other by a jagged-line symbol worn somewhere on their person. Most members are young, angry, and genuinely grieved by the system's failures. A small number are dangerous. A smaller number are calculating. The movement has been growing for two years in Murano's outer districts, and Class 1-A are going to become symbols in that conflict whether they want to be or not.
factions.structure: Horizontal by design, hierarchical in practice. The Fracture Line — three charismatic figures who founded the movement — function as de facto leadership while maintaining public deniability. Below them, Cell Leaders coordinate local action. Most members never meet anyone above their cell. The ideology holds cells together more than any command structure does.
factions.public_awareness: Visible graffiti and minor incidents from Tier 1. A known named movement by Tier 2 — the Commission is watching. Direct clash with hero infrastructure in Tier 3. By Tier 4, Fracture forces Class 1-A into a moral question: are the people they're fighting actually wrong?

villains.name: Maki Torune
villains.role: Co-founder of Fracture. The one who writes the manifestos. The voice.
villains.quirk_summary: Shard can produce large, razor-edged blades directly from her body. She can form them into sweeping arcs or single strikes, slicing through flesh and steel alike. At full mastery she can extend the blades in a wide sweeping arc to cut down multiple targets at once. She bleeds and strains her muscles when she overuses her quirk — a cost she has stopped noticing.

quirk_mechanics.weakness: Prolonged use severely strains her muscles — after using the Quirk for more than one minute continuously, she must succeed on a DC 15 Constitution save or take 1d12 true damage.

abilities.name: Blade Strike
abilities.description: Produces a razor-sharp blade from her arm and strikes a creature within 5 feet, dealing 2d12 slashing damage (double against constructs) and inflicting 1 rank of Bleed.

abilities.name: Razor Sweep
abilities.description: Extends her arm blades in a sweeping arc, striking all creatures in a 30-foot line. Each target makes a Dexterity save against her Quirk save DC or takes 2d12 slashing damage (half on success) and gains 2 ranks of Bleed. After the attack she may move to the far end of the line.

villains.appearance: Late 20s. Cuts on her forearms that she's stopped trying to cover. Fracture's jagged-line symbol tattooed on her collarbone. Speaks like someone who's rehearsed everything and means all of it.
villains.personality: True believer. Not naive — she knows Fracture has done things she can't fully defend. She believes the alternative is worse. She is smart, magnetic, and completely unwilling to compromise on the core premise. The class may find her hard to argue with and that's intentional.
villains.combat_style: Close-to-mid range area denial through blade arcs rather than ranged attacks. She keeps enemies at the edge of her reach with sweeping strikes, forcing them to either close in and eat a Blade Strike or hang back and let her reposition. She targets support heroes and quirkless members first — not out of malice but strategy. She wants to end fights before someone gets killed.

special_abilities: Blade Strike (Scaled): Melee attack +7 to hit. On a hit, 3d8 slashing damage and 1 rank of Bleed. Can strike twice on her turn if both targets are within 5 ft of each other.

Razor Sweep (Scaled): As an action, extends her arm blades in a sweeping arc, striking all creatures in a 25-ft line. DEX save DC 16 or take 4d8 slashing damage and 2 ranks of Bleed (half damage, no Bleed on success). She may move to the far end of the line afterward.
Guard Blades: As a reaction, raises crossed forearm blades to block an incoming melee attack, reducing the damage by 2d8 plus her proficiency bonus.

Crimson Arc: Recharge 5-6. All creatures in a 30 ft radius make DEX save DC 16 or take 6d8 slashing damage and begin Bleeding (1d6 at start of each turn until they use an action to staunch it). Half damage on success, no Bleed. Afterward she must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or take 1d12 true damage from the strain.

campaign_appearances.tier_1: Name and symbol appear in graffiti and leaflets. One Cell Leader may mention her name with reverence.
campaign_appearances.tier_2: First direct encounter — she's at a Fracture rally that turns into a confrontation. She doesn't want to fight the class. She wants to debate them.
campaign_appearances.tier_3: The confrontation becomes unavoidable. She's protecting people the system has failed. Is she wrong?
campaign_appearances.tier_4: The class must decide: is Fracture a villain organisation, or a symptom? Shard is the face of that question.

villains.dm_notes: Every time she talks to the class, give her a point. She should land real arguments. The fight with her shouldn't feel clean. If a player says 'she's not wrong,' that's exactly right.
villains.season_1_role: Met directly in The Fracture Problem (Tier 2) and present on the rooftop in Convergence. Combat is avoidable — designed as moral confrontation. If combat happens, use full stat block. She will not fight the class in Convergence unless they actively come after her.

villains.name: Daisuke Ren
villains.role: Fracture's most capable field operative. Co-founder. The one who handles problems Shard's words can't solve.
villains.quirk_summary: Voltage generates and discharges high-voltage electricity through his body and nearby conductive surfaces. Unlike standard Electrification quirks, his carries a paralytic charge that disrupts muscle control. He can regulate output with precision — from a numbing tingle to a strike that knocks a person unconscious in one hit.
villains.appearance: Early 30s. Scarred fingers, slightly singed hair that regrows burnt. Calm in a way that reads as dangerous — he doesn't get agitated before a fight. The Fracture symbol on the back of his jacket.
villains.personality: Quiet and direct. He doesn't share Shard's gift for argument but he doesn't need to. He's there to protect the movement and its people, and he takes that seriously enough to fight heroes who are, in his view, protecting an unjust system.
villains.combat_style: Crowd control into shutdown. He uses the environment — metal fences, wet ground, support equipment — as extensions of his quirk. He tries to disable rather than kill. If the class has fought him before, he's adapted.

special_abilities: Shock Strike: Melee attack +6 to hit. 2d8+3 lightning damage. Target makes DC 16 CON save or has their movement halved until end of their next turn (muscle disruption).

Chain Discharge: As an action, discharge through a conductive surface (metal, water, wet ground). All creatures within 15 ft of the surface make DC 16 DEX save or take 3d8 lightning damage and are Stunned until end of their next turn. Half damage on success, not stunned.
Paralytic Surge: Once per short rest. One creature Voltage can touch makes DC 16 CON save or is Paralysed for 1 minute. They repeat the save at the end of each turn.
Grounded: Voltage is immune to lightning damage and has advantage on saves against quirks that affect the nervous system.

campaign_appearances.tier_1: Unknown. May be present at Fracture events but not identified.
campaign_appearances.tier_2: Identified after a Fracture incident where he intervenes to stop a situation from escalating to casualties — a hero move from a villain.
campaign_appearances.tier_3: Direct confrontation. He's protecting Fracture members from a Hero Commission crackdown. He and the class may be on the same side for thirty seconds before it goes wrong.
campaign_appearances.tier_4: If Shard is cornered, Voltage is the last line. He won't surrender. He will try to get her out.

villains.dm_notes: He's the tactician. He reads the room, reads the class. Use him to punish overconfidence — he knows that Hellflame can't run electricity through its own fire and will take advantage of it.
villains.season_1_role: Met directly in The Fracture Problem (Tier 2) — performs a hero move (protecting an elderly protester) while being a villain. Present but non-combative in Convergence, ensuring Shard's exit is clean. If forced into combat, use full stat block.

quirk_mechanics.weakness: Stores up to 1,000,000 watts of electricity, which builds as he uses the Quirk. As wattage rises his Intelligence drops and movement speed halves, culminating in unconsciousness at 1,000,000 watts.

abilities.name: Electrification
abilities.description: Melee or ranged attack (30 feet) dealing 2d8 lightning damage. Costs 5,001 watts. As a reaction, he can also absorb lightning damage dealt to himself or an ally within 20 feet, reducing it to 0 and gaining resistance to lightning damage.

abilities.name: Human Stun Gun
abilities.description: Discharges electricity in a 10-foot radius or 15-foot line. Creatures must succeed on a Constitution save or take 2d8 lightning damage, become Stunned until the end of his next turn, and gain a rank of Shocked (half damage, no Stun on a success). Costs 10,001 watts.
